infobox.value: * [Peter Fox](/wiki/Peter_Fox_(Welsh_politician) "Peter Fox (Welsh politician)") ([C](/wiki/Welsh_Conservatives "Welsh Conservatives")) * [John Griffiths](/wiki/John_Griffiths_(Welsh_politician) "John Griffiths (Welsh politician)") ([L](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour")) +4 [regional members](/wiki/South_Wales_East_(Senedd_electoral_region) "South Wales East (Senedd electoral region)")

([/ˈmɒnməθʃər, ˈmʌn-, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Sir Fynwy*) is a [county](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") in the [south east](/wiki/South_East_Wales "South East Wales") of [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). It borders [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys") to the north; the English counties of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") to the north and east; the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary") to the south, and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") and [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent") to the west. The largest town is [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny"), and the administrative centre is [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk").

**Monmouthshire** ([/ˈmɒnməθʃər, ˈmʌn-, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Sir Fynwy*) is a [county](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") in the [south east](/wiki/South_East_Wales "South East Wales") of [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). It borders [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys") to the north; the English counties of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") to the north and east; the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary") to the south, and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") and [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent") to the west. The largest town is [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny"), and the administrative centre is [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk").

The county is rural, although adjacent to the city of Newport and the urbanised [South Wales Valleys](/wiki/South_Wales_Valleys "South Wales Valleys"). It has an area of 330 square miles (850 km2) and a population of 94,930. Abergavenny is located in the north-east, and Usk in near the centre; other settlements include [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth") in the east, [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow "Chepstow") in the south-east, [Caldicot](/wiki/Caldicot "Caldicot") in the south. [Monmouthshire County Council](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_Council "Monmouthshire County Council") is the local authority. Monmouthshire was established in 1996 and is named after the [historic county of the same name](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)"), of which it covers approximately the eastern three-fifths. The county has one of the lowest percentages of [Welsh speakers](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") in Wales, at 8.2% of the population in 2021.

The lowlands in the centre of Monmouthshire are gently undulating, and shaped by the [River Usk](/wiki/River_Usk "River Usk") and its tributaries. The west of the county is hilly, and the [Black Mountains](/wiki/Black_Mountains,_Wales "Black Mountains, Wales") in the north-west are part of the [Brecon Beacons National Park](/wiki/Brecon_Beacons_National_Park "Brecon Beacons National Park"). The border with England in the east largely follows the course of the [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye") and its tributary, the [River Monnow](/wiki/River_Monnow "River Monnow"). In the south east is the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley"), a hilly region which stretches into England and which has been designated an [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty"). The county has a shoreline on the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary"), with crossings into England by the [Severn Bridge](/wiki/Severn_Bridge "Severn Bridge") and [Prince of Wales Bridge](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Bridge "Prince of Wales Bridge").

The [Gwent Levels](/wiki/Gwent_Levels "Gwent Levels") in the south of the county contain signs of human occupation dating back eight [millennia](/wiki/Millennium "Millennium"). During the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age"), south-east Wales was settled by the [Silures](/wiki/Silures "Silures"), who opposed the [Roman conquest of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain") before being subdued. The ruins of [Venta Silurum](/wiki/Venta_Silurum "Venta Silurum"), in modern-day [Caerwent](/wiki/Caerwent "Caerwent"), are evidence of Roman rule. The sub-Roman history of the county is poorly documented but saw the founding of petty kingdoms, including [Gwent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwent "Kingdom of Gwent"). The area was part of the [Welsh Marches](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches") during the Middle Ages and was frequently contested, as reflected in its "fine collection" of castles. In the seventeenth century, [Raglan Castle](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle") was among the last [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") strongholds to fall to [Parliamentarian](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") forces in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), and the county became a [recusant stronghold](/wiki/Recusancy "Recusancy") in which Catholics were widely persecuted. In the mid-19th century, [John Frost](/wiki/John_Frost_(Chartist) "John Frost (Chartist)") and other [Chartist](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") leaders were tried and sentenced to death at the [Shire Hall, Monmouth](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Monmouth "Shire Hall, Monmouth") after the "first mass movement of the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class")" in Britain. At the same time, the [Wye Tour](/wiki/Wye_Tour "Wye Tour") and the ruins [Tintern Abbey](/wiki/Tintern_Abbey "Tintern Abbey") drew tourists to the county. In the 21st century the economy is based on the [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector"), agriculture and tourism.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Hillfort%2C_Skirrid_Fawr%2C_Llantilio_Pertholey.JPG/250px-Hillfort%2C_Skirrid_Fawr%2C_Llantilio_Pertholey.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hillfort,_Skirrid_Fawr,_Llantilio_Pertholey.JPG)

[Hillfort](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") on the summit of [Skirrid Fawr](/wiki/Skirrid_Fawr "Skirrid Fawr")

Main article: [Prehistoric Wales](/wiki/Prehistoric_Wales "Prehistoric Wales")

Evidence of human activity in the [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") period has been found across Monmouthshire; examples include remains on the [Caldicot and Wentloog Levels](/wiki/Caldicot_and_Wentloog_Levels "Caldicot and Wentloog Levels")[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[a]](#cite_note-6) and at [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_in_the_Mesolithic_period "Monmouth in the Mesolithic period").[[6]](#cite_note-7) A major hoard of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") [axes](/wiki/Axe "Axe") was discovered at [St Arvans](/wiki/St_Arvans "St Arvans").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198817-8) The county has a number of [hillfort sites](/wiki/List_of_hillforts_in_Monmouthshire "List of hillforts in Monmouthshire"), such as those at [Bulwark](/wiki/Bulwark,_Chepstow#Bulwarks_Camp "Bulwark, Chepstow")[[8]](#cite_note-9) and [Llanmelin Wood](/wiki/Llanvair_Discoed#Llanmelin_hill_fort "Llanvair Discoed").[[9]](#cite_note-10) The latter has been suggested as the capital of the [Silures](/wiki/Silures "Silures"), a [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") tribe who occupied south-east Wales in the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").[[10]](#cite_note-11) The Silures proved among the most intractable of Rome's opponents; [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus") described them as "exceptionally stubborn" and Raymond Howell, in his county history published in 1988, notes that while it took the Romans five years to subdue south-east England, it took thirty-five before complete subjugation of the Silurian territories was achieved.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198825-12)

### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roman period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Four_Seasons_mosaic.jpg/330px-Four_Seasons_mosaic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Four_Seasons_mosaic.jpg)

Four Seasons mosaic from a *[domus](/wiki/Domus "Domus")* at [Venta Silurum](/wiki/Venta_Silurum "Venta Silurum")

Main article: [Wales in the Roman era](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Roman_era "Wales in the Roman era")

The [Roman conquest of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain") began in AD 43, and within five years the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire")'s [legions](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion") had reached the borders of what is now Wales.[[12]](#cite_note-13) In south east Wales they encountered strong resistance from the Silures, led by [Caratacus](/wiki/Caratacus "Caratacus") (Caradog), who had fled west after the defeat of his own tribe, the [Catuvellauni](/wiki/Catuvellauni "Catuvellauni"). His [final defeat](/wiki/Caratacus%27_last_battle "Caratacus' last battle") in AD 50 saw his transportation to Rome, but Silurian resistance continued, and the subjugation of the entirety of south east Wales was not achieved until around AD 75, under the governor of Britain, [Sextus Julius Frontinus](/wiki/Frontinus "Frontinus").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198825-12)

Monmouthshire's most important Roman remains are found at the town of [Venta Silurum](/wiki/Venta_Silurum "Venta Silurum") ("Market of the Silures"), present-day [Caerwent](/wiki/Caerwent "Caerwent") in the south of the county. The town was established in AD 75,[[13]](#cite_note-14) laid out in the traditional rectangular Roman pattern of twenty [insulae](/wiki/Insula_(Roman_city) "Insula (Roman city)") with a [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") and a temple flanking a [forum](/wiki/Forum_(Roman) "Forum (Roman)").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198829-15) Other Roman settlements in the area included [Blestium](/wiki/Blestium "Blestium") (Monmouth).[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[b]](#cite_note-18) The [Romanisation](/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)") of Monmouthshire was not without continuing civil unrest; the defences at Caerwent, and at Caerleon, underwent considerable strengthening in the late [2nd century](/wiki/2nd_century "2nd century") in response to disturbances. The Silurian identity was not extinguished: the establishment of a *Respublica Civitatis Silurium* (an early town council) at the beginning of the [3rd century](/wiki/3rd_century "3rd century") testifies to the longevity of the indigenous tribal culture.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198830-19)

### Sub-Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sub-Roman period")]

Main article: [Kingdom of Gwent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwent "Kingdom of Gwent")

The [Roman abandonment of Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") saw the division of Wales into a number of [petty kingdoms](/wiki/Petty_kingdoms "Petty kingdoms"). In the south east (the present county of Monmouthshire) the [Kingdom of Gwent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwent "Kingdom of Gwent") was established, traditionally ascribed to [Caradoc](/wiki/Caradoc "Caradoc"), in the 5th or 6th centuries. Its capital, Caerwent, gave the name to the kingdom.[[18]](#cite_note-20) The subsequent history of the area prior to the Norman Conquest is poorly documented and complex. The Kingdom of Gwent frequently fought with the neighbouring Welsh kingdoms, and sometimes joined in alliance with them in, generally successful, attempts to repel the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain"), their common enemy. The [Book of Llandaff](/wiki/Book_of_Llandaff "Book of Llandaff") records such a victory over the Saxon invaders achieved by [Tewdrig](/wiki/Tewdrig "Tewdrig") at a battle near [Tintern](/wiki/Tintern "Tintern") in the late 6th century.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198840-21)[[c]](#cite_note-23) An example of the alliances formed by neighbouring petty kings was the [Kingdom of Morgannwg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Morgannwg "Kingdom of Morgannwg"), a union between Gwent and its western neighbour, the Kingdom of [Glywysing](/wiki/Glywysing "Glywysing"), which formed and reformed between the 8th and the 10th centuries.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198841-24) The common threat they faced is shown in [Offa's Dyke](/wiki/Offa%27s_Dyke "Offa's Dyke"), the physical delineation of a border with Wales created by the [Mercian](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") [king](/wiki/Offa "Offa").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198845-25)[[d]](#cite_note-27) For a brief period in the 11th century, Monmouthshire, as Gwent, became part of a united Wales under [Gruffydd ap Llywelyn](/wiki/Gruffydd_ap_Llywelyn "Gruffydd ap Llywelyn"), but his death in 1063 was soon followed by that of his opponent [Harold Godwinson](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson") at the [Battle of Hastings](/wiki/Battle_of_Hastings "Battle of Hastings"), and the re-established unity of the country was to come from Norman dominance.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198847-28)

### Norman period and Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Norman period and Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Chepstow_castleS2_stitch.jpg/330px-Chepstow_castleS2_stitch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chepstow_castleS2_stitch.jpg)

[Chepstow Castle](/wiki/Chepstow_Castle "Chepstow Castle") – stronghold of the [Lords of Striguil](/wiki/Marcher_lord "Marcher lord") and "the glory of medieval south Wales"

Main article: [Welsh Marches](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches")

The [Norman invasion of South Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales") from the late 1060s saw the destruction of the Kingdom of Gwent,[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1992100–102-29) and its replacement by five [Marcher lordships](/wiki/Marcher_Lords "Marcher Lords") based at [Striguil](/wiki/Striguil "Striguil") (Chepstow), Monmouth, Abergavenny, Usk and Caerleon.[[26]](#cite_note-30) The Marcher Lord of Abergavenny, [Gilbert de Clare, 7th Earl of Gloucester](/wiki/Gilbert_de_Clare,_7th_Earl_of_Gloucester "Gilbert de Clare, 7th Earl of Gloucester"), described the rule of the lords as *sicut regale* ("like unto a king").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1966?-31) The lords established castles, first earth and wood [motte-and-bailey](/wiki/Motte-and-bailey "Motte-and-bailey") constructions, and later substantial structures in stone. Among the first were [Chepstow Castle](/wiki/Chepstow_Castle "Chepstow Castle"), begun by [William FitzOsbern, 1st Earl of Hereford](/wiki/William_FitzOsbern,_1st_Earl_of_Hereford "William FitzOsbern, 1st Earl of Hereford") as early as 1067,[[28]](#cite_note-32) and that at [Tregrug](/wiki/Tregrug_Castle "Tregrug Castle"), near [Llangybi](/wiki/Llangybi,_Monmouthshire "Llangybi, Monmouthshire"), by de Clare's son, [Gilbert](/wiki/Gilbert_de_Clare,_8th_Earl_of_Gloucester "Gilbert de Clare, 8th Earl of Gloucester").[[29]](#cite_note-33) The historian John Kenyon describes Chepstow as "one of the great strongholds of Europe".[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon2010113-34)[[e]](#cite_note-35) In the early Norman period, the cleric and chronicler, [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth") (c. 1095 – c. 1155), who may have been born at Monmouth, wrote his *[The History of the Kings of Britain](/wiki/The_History_of_the_Kings_of_Britain "The History of the Kings of Britain")*, with a focus on [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur") and on his capital, [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot "Camelot"), which Geoffrey located at [Caerleon](/wiki/Caerleon "Caerleon") (now in [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales")), and which remained highly influential for centuries, although modern scholars consider it little more than a literary forgery.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright1984xxviii-36)[[f]](#cite_note-38)

Christmas 1175 saw an outbreak of particular violence in the gradual extension of Norman control over South Wales. The Marcher lord [William de Braose](/wiki/William_de_Braose,_3rd_Lord_of_Bramber "William de Braose, 3rd Lord of Bramber") invited [Seisyll ap Dyfnwal](/wiki/Seisyll_ap_Dyfnwal "Seisyll ap Dyfnwal"), lord of Upper Gwent, and an array of other Welsh notables to a feast at [Abergavenny Castle](/wiki/Abergavenny_Castle "Abergavenny Castle"). De Braose proceeded to have his men massacre the Welsh, before sending them to burn Seisyll's home at [Castell Arnallt](/wiki/Castell_Arnallt "Castell Arnallt") and to murder his son, intending the obliteration of the indigenous Gwent aristocracy. A wave of Welsh retaliation followed, described in detail by the contemporary chronicler, [Gerald of Wales](/wiki/Gerald_of_Wales "Gerald of Wales").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198858-39)

Monmouthshire's Norman castles later became favoured residences of the [Plantagenet nobility](/wiki/House_of_Plantagenet "House of Plantagenet"). [Henry of Grosmont, Duke of Lancaster](/wiki/Henry_of_Grosmont,_Duke_of_Lancaster "Henry of Grosmont, Duke of Lancaster") (c. 1310–1361), was reputedly born at [Grosmont Castle](/wiki/Grosmont_Castle "Grosmont Castle"),[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrmrod2005-40) home of his father [Henry, 3rd Earl of Lancaster](/wiki/Henry,_3rd_Earl_of_Lancaster "Henry, 3rd Earl of Lancaster"), grandson of [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"). Becoming the richest and among the most powerful lords in England, Grosmont developed the castle as a sumptuous residence, while the village became an important medieval settlement.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight200911-41) [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") (1386–1422) was born at his father's castle at [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_Castle "Monmouth Castle") in 1386,[[g]](#cite_note-43) and his birth, and his most famous [military victory](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt"), are commemorated in [Agincourt Square](/wiki/Agincourt_Square "Agincourt Square") in the town, and by a statue on the frontage of the [Shire Hall](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Monmouth "Shire Hall, Monmouth") which forms the square's centrepiece.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllmand2010-44)[[h]](#cite_note-49) In Henry V's wars in France, he received strong military support from the archers of Gwent, who were famed for their skill with the [Welsh bow](/wiki/Welsh_bow "Welsh bow"). Gerald recorded, "the men of Gwent are more skilled with the bow and arrow than those who come from other parts of Wales".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198862-50)[[i]](#cite_note-52)

There was a brief reassertion of Welsh autonomy in Monmouthshire during the [Glyndŵr rebellion](/wiki/Glynd%C5%B5r_rebellion "Glyndŵr rebellion") of 1400 to 1415. Seeking to re-establish Welsh independence, the revolt began in the north, but by 1403 [Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr")'s army was in Monmouthshire, sacking [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk")[[44]](#cite_note-53) and securing a victory over the English at [Craig-y-dorth](/wiki/Cwmcarvan#Craig-y-dorth "Cwmcarvan"), near [Cwmcarvan](/wiki/Cwmcarvan "Cwmcarvan"). According to the *Annals of Owain Glyn Dwr*, "there the English were killed for the most part and they were pursued up to the gates of the town" (of Monmouth).[[45]](#cite_note-auto4-54)[[j]](#cite_note-55) This was the high water mark of the revolt; heavy defeats in the county followed in 1405, at the [Battle of Grosmont](/wiki/Battle_of_Grosmont "Battle of Grosmont"), and at the [Battle of Pwll Melyn](/wiki/Battle_of_Pwll_Melyn "Battle of Pwll Melyn"), traditionally located near [Usk Castle](/wiki/Usk_Castle "Usk Castle"), where Glyndŵr's brother was killed and his eldest son captured. The chronicler [Adam of Usk](/wiki/Adam_of_Usk "Adam of Usk"), a contemporary observer, noted that "from this time onward, Owain's fortunes began to wane in that region."[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas-Symonds200414-56)

### Monmouthshire 1535–1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Monmouthshire 1535–1974")]

Main article: [Monmouthshire (historic)](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)")

#### Tudor reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tudor reforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Monumetha_Atlas.jpg/250px-Monumetha_Atlas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monumetha_Atlas.jpg)

Hand-drawn map of Monmouthshire by [Christopher Saxton](/wiki/Christopher_Saxton "Christopher Saxton") from 1577

The first [Tudor](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor") king, [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"), was born at [Pembroke Castle](/wiki/Pembroke_Castle "Pembroke Castle") in the west of Wales, and spent some of his childhood in Monmouthshire, at [Raglan Castle](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle") as a ward of [William Herbert, 1st Earl of Pembroke](/wiki/William_Herbert,_1st_Earl_of_Pembroke_(died_1469) "William Herbert, 1st Earl of Pembroke (died 1469)").[[47]](#cite_note-57) His son and heir [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") was to bring the rule of the [Marcher lords](/wiki/Marcher_lords "Marcher lords") to an end. The historic county of Monmouthshire was formed from the [Welsh Marches](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches") by the [Laws in Wales Act 1535](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542"). The Laws in Wales Act 1542 enumerated the counties of Wales and omitted Monmouthshire, implying that the county was no longer to be treated as part of Wales. Though for all purposes Wales had become part of the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), and the difference had little practical effect, it did begin a centuries-long dispute as to Monmouthshire's status as a *Welsh* or as an *English* county, a debate only finally brought to an end in 1972.[[48]](#cite_note-58)

The laws establishing the 13 counties (shires), the [historic counties of Wales](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_Wales "Historic counties of Wales"),[[49]](#cite_note-59) assigned four of the five new counties created from the Marcher Lordships along the Welsh/English border, [Brecknockshire](/wiki/Brecknockshire "Brecknockshire"), [Denbighshire](/wiki/Denbighshire "Denbighshire"), [Montgomeryshire](/wiki/Montgomeryshire "Montgomeryshire") and [Radnorshire](/wiki/Radnorshire "Radnorshire"), to the legal system operated in Wales, administered by the [Court of Great Sessions](/wiki/Court_of_Great_Sessions_in_Wales "Court of Great Sessions in Wales"). Monmouthshire was assigned to the [Oxford circuit](/wiki/Oxford_circuit "Oxford circuit") of the [English Assizes](/wiki/Assize_Court#England_and_Wales "Assize Court").[[50]](#cite_note-60) This began a legal separation which continued until 1972; for example, the administrative county of Monmouthshire and the boroughs of Newport, Abergavenny and Monmouth were explicitly listed as being in England rather than Wales in first schedule of the [Local Government Act 1933](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1933 "Local Government Act 1933"). For several centuries, acts of the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") (in which Wales was represented) often referred to "Wales and Monmouthshire", such as the [Welsh Church Act 1914](/wiki/Welsh_Church_Act_1914 "Welsh Church Act 1914").[[51]](#cite_note-61)

#### Civil war and religious strife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Civil war and religious strife")]

Monmouthshire in the 1600s experienced to a high degree the political and religious convulsions arising from the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") and culminating in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). Following [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England")'s religious reforms, the county had a reputation for [recusancy](/wiki/Recusancy "Recusancy"), with the strongly Catholic [Marquesses of Worcester](/wiki/Duke_of_Beaufort "Duke of Beaufort") (later Dukes of Beaufort) at its apex, from their powerbase at [Raglan Castle](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle").[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins1986562-62) The outbreak of war saw the county predominantly [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") in its sympathies; [Henry Somerset, 1st Marquess of Worcester](/wiki/Henry_Somerset,_1st_Marquess_of_Worcester "Henry Somerset, 1st Marquess of Worcester") expended a fortune in support of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") and twice entertained him at Raglan. His generosity was unavailing; the castle fell after a siege in 1646; the marquess died in captivity and his [son](/wiki/Edward_Somerset,_2nd_Marquess_of_Worcester "Edward Somerset, 2nd Marquess of Worcester") spent time in prison and in exile abroad.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETribe20024–6-63)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon200319-64)

[John Arnold](/wiki/John_Arnold_of_Monmouthshire "John Arnold of Monmouthshire"), [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of parliament (United Kingdom)") for [Monmouth Boroughs](/wiki/Monmouth_Boroughs_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouth Boroughs (UK Parliament constituency)") in the late 17th century, was a virulent opponent of Catholics and pursued a policy of harassment from the 1670s.[[55]](#cite_note-65) Monmouthshire's only [dukedom](/wiki/Duke "Duke") was created in 1663 for [James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth"), but became forfeit following Scott's execution after the failed [Monmouth Rebellion](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion") in 1685.[[56]](#cite_note-66) In the 18th and much of the 19th centuries county politics was dominated by the Beauforts and the [Morgans](/wiki/Baron_Tredegar "Baron Tredegar"), "an everlasting friendship between the houses of [Raglan](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle") and [Tredegar](/wiki/Tredegar_House "Tredegar House")",[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins1986568-67) and by the end of the 19th century, these two families, along with the [Hanburys](/wiki/John_Hanbury_(1664%E2%80%931734) "John Hanbury (1664–1734)") of Pontypool, held between them over a fifth of the total land in the county.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins1986574-68)

#### Industrialisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Industrialisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/John_Frost%2C_%28the_chartist.%29_Found_guilty_of_high_treason_at_Monmouth_and_sentenced_to_death_16_Jany_1840_LCCN2003672983.jpg/250px-John_Frost%2C_%28the_chartist.%29_Found_guilty_of_high_treason_at_Monmouth_and_sentenced_to_death_16_Jany_1840_LCCN2003672983.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Frost,_(the_chartist.)_Found_guilty_of_high_treason_at_Monmouth_and_sentenced_to_death_16_Jany_1840_LCCN2003672983.jpg)

[John Frost](/wiki/John_Frost_(Chartist) "John Frost (Chartist)") was sentenced to death at Monmouth after the failure of the [Newport Rising](/wiki/Newport_Rising "Newport Rising")

Industrialisation came early to Monmouthshire; the first [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") in Britain was produced at a [foundry](/wiki/Foundry "Foundry") at [Tintern](/wiki/Tintern "Tintern") in 1568,[[59]](#cite_note-69) and the lower Wye Valley and the [Forest of Dean](/wiki/Forest_of_Dean "Forest of Dean") became important centres for metalworking and mining. But the most dramatic impact was in the west of the county during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution_in_Wales "Industrial Revolution in Wales"), in the [South Wales Coalfield](/wiki/South_Wales_Coalfield "South Wales Coalfield"), where some of the largest pits in Wales were dug, and a major [iron industry](/wiki/Ironworks "Ironworks") developed.[[60]](#cite_note-70) The societal transformation was accompanied by great inequality and unrest. [Chartism](/wiki/Chartism_in_Wales "Chartism in Wales") was firmly embedded in Wales, and in 1840 the Chartist leaders [John Frost](/wiki/John_Frost_(Chartist) "John Frost (Chartist)"), [Zephaniah Williams](/wiki/Zephaniah_Williams "Zephaniah Williams") and [William Jones](/wiki/William_Jones_(Chartist) "William Jones (Chartist)") were tried for sedition and treason at the [Shire Hall, Monmouth](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Monmouth "Shire Hall, Monmouth"), after a [failed insurrection at Newport](/wiki/Newport_Rising "Newport Rising"), which the writer [Clive Aslet](/wiki/Clive_Aslet "Clive Aslet") called "the first mass movement of the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class")".[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAslet2005428-71) Their death sentences were subsequently commuted to [transportation](/wiki/Transportation "Transportation") to Australia.[[62]](#cite_note-72)

Industrialisation also drove improvements in transportation; in the 18th century, the poor state of Monmouthshire's roads approached a national scandal. During a debate in parliament on the establishment of a [turnpike trust](/wiki/Turnpike_trust "Turnpike trust") for the county, the local landowner [Valentine Morris](/wiki/Valentine_Morris "Valentine Morris") asserted that the inhabitants of the county travelled "in ditches".[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeeks2009243-73) By the mid-century, commercial demands saw the first timetabled [stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach "Stagecoach") between London and Monmouth arrive in [Agincourt Square](/wiki/Agincourt_Square "Agincourt Square") on 4 November 1763, the journey having taken four days.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeeks2009245-74) By the end of the century, the need for access to exploit the South Wales Coalfields saw the development of trams and canals.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeeks2009249-75)

#### Society, art and science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Society, art and science")]

Tourism became prominent in Monmouthshire at the end of the 18th century, when the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and the subsequent [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") precluded travel to [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrews198986-76) The focus of activity was the [Wye Tour](/wiki/Wye_Tour "Wye Tour"), first popularised by the Rev. [William Gilpin](/wiki/William_Gilpin_(priest) "William Gilpin (priest)"), in his *Observations on the River Wye and Several Parts of South Wales, etc. Relative Chiefly to Picturesque Beauty*, published in 1782. Although his efforts were sometimes satirised, Gilpin established what became the conventional route down the "mazy course" of the [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye"), with visitors embarking at [Ross-on-Wye](/wiki/Ross-on-Wye "Ross-on-Wye"), and sailing past [Symonds Yat](/wiki/Symonds_Yat "Symonds Yat"), and Monmouth, before the highlight of the tour, [Tintern Abbey](/wiki/Tintern_Abbey "Tintern Abbey").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilpin178217-77) Voyages concluded at Chepstow. The abbey at Tintern inspired artists and writers; [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") painted it;[[68]](#cite_note-78) [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth") committed it to verse;[[69]](#cite_note-79) and [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") almost died there.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECottle183748-80) Another object of interest to artists undertaking the Wye Tour was the [Monnow Bridge](/wiki/Monnow_Bridge "Monnow Bridge") at Monmouth.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell2010106-81) A late 18th-century watercolour by [Michael Angelo Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Angelo_Rooker "Michael Angelo Rooker") is now in the [Monmouth Museum](/wiki/Monmouth_Museum "Monmouth Museum").[[72]](#cite_note-82) The noted architectural watercolourist [Samuel Prout](/wiki/Samuel_Prout "Samuel Prout") painted the bridge in a study dated "before 1814", now held at the [Yale Center for British Art](/wiki/Yale_Center_for_British_Art "Yale Center for British Art") in Connecticut.[[73]](#cite_note-83) In 1795, [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") sketched the bridge and gatehouse during one of his annual summer sketching tours.[[74]](#cite_note-84)

[Alfred Russel Wallace](/wiki/Alfred_Russel_Wallace "Alfred Russel Wallace"), a [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalist "Naturalist") whose independent work on [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") saw [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") bring forward the publication of *[On the Origin of Species](/wiki/On_the_Origin_of_Species "On the Origin of Species")*, was born at [Llanbadoc](/wiki/Llanbadoc "Llanbadoc"), outside [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk"), in 1823. He is commemorated in a statue raised in the town's Twyn Square in 2021.[[75]](#cite_note-85) [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), the philosopher and the only [Nobel laureate](/wiki/Nobel_laureate "Nobel laureate") from the county, was born at [Cleddon Hall](/wiki/Cleddon_Hall "Cleddon Hall"), outside [Trellech](/wiki/Trellech "Trellech") in 1872.[[76]](#cite_note-86) [Charles Rolls](/wiki/Charles_Rolls "Charles Rolls") grew up at his family seat, [The Hendre](/wiki/The_Hendre "The Hendre"), just north of Monmouth and, in partnership with [Henry Royce](/wiki/Henry_Royce "Henry Royce"), co-founded [Rolls-Royce Limited](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Limited "Rolls-Royce Limited"). An aviation pioneer, as well as an automobile innovator, he died in a plane crash in 1910,[[77]](#cite_note-87) and is commemorated by a [statue](/wiki/Statue_of_Charles_Rolls,_Monmouth "Statue of Charles Rolls, Monmouth") in Agincourt Square in Monmouth.[[78]](#cite_note-88)

#### War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/HMS_Monmouth.jpg/250px-HMS_Monmouth.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Monmouth.jpg)

[HMS *Monmouth*](/wiki/HMS_Monmouth_(1901) "HMS Monmouth (1901)"), November 1914

The [Royal Monmouthshire Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Monmouthshire_Royal_Engineers "Royal Monmouthshire Royal Engineers") was founded in 1539, making it the second-oldest regiment in the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"). Originally a county militia, it was amalgamated into the [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") in 1877. It is based at [Monmouth Castle](/wiki/Monmouth_Castle "Monmouth Castle").[[79]](#cite_note-89) [Fitzroy Somerset](/wiki/FitzRoy_Somerset,_1st_Baron_Raglan "FitzRoy Somerset, 1st Baron Raglan"), a younger son of the [5th Duke of Beaufort](/wiki/Henry_Somerset,_5th_Duke_of_Beaufort "Henry Somerset, 5th Duke of Beaufort"), enjoyed a long military career, serving on the staff of the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"),[[80]](#cite_note-90) and as commander-in-chief of the British forces during the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradney199241–42-91) Created [Baron Raglan](/wiki/Baron_Raglan "Baron Raglan") in 1852, he died in 1855. His son was gifted [Cefntilla Court](/wiki/Cefntilla_Court "Cefntilla Court"), near [Llandenny](/wiki/Llandenny "Llandenny") in his memory.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2000272–273-92) [William Wilson Allen](/wiki/William_Allen_(VC_1879) "William Allen (VC 1879)"), who fought with the [South Wales Borderers](/wiki/South_Wales_Borderers "South Wales Borderers") at the [Battle of Rorke's Drift](/wiki/Battle_of_Rorke%27s_Drift "Battle of Rorke's Drift") in 1879, is buried in [Monmouth Cemetery](/wiki/Monmouth_Cemetery "Monmouth Cemetery"), the only grave in the county of a holder of the [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross").[[83]](#cite_note-93)[[84]](#cite_note-94)[[k]](#cite_note-98) The [Monmouthshire Regiment](/wiki/Monmouthshire_Regiment "Monmouthshire Regiment") was established in 1907. Men from the regiment fought in both the [First](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), until its disbandment in 1967.[[88]](#cite_note-99) [HMS *Monmouth*](/wiki/HMS_Monmouth_(1901) "HMS Monmouth (1901)") was sunk at the [Battle of Coronel](/wiki/Battle_of_Coronel "Battle of Coronel") in November 1914, with the loss of all 734 crew.[[89]](#cite_note-100)

### Gwent 1974–1996

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Gwent 1974–1996")]

Main article: [Gwent (county)](/wiki/Gwent_(county) "Gwent (county)")

The [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), which came into effect in April 1974, created the county of Gwent, confirmed it as part of Wales, and abolished the historic [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county") of Monmouthshire and its associated [lieutenancy](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Monmouthshire "Lord Lieutenant of Monmouthshire"). It also subsumed [Newport County Borough Council](/wiki/Newport_County_Borough_Council "Newport County Borough Council"), creating a two-tier system of local government across the county. The entire county was administered by [Gwent County Council](/wiki/Gwent_County_Council "Gwent County Council"), based at [County Hall, Cwmbran](/wiki/County_Hall,_Cwmbran "County Hall, Cwmbran"), with five [district councils](/wiki/History_of_local_government_in_Wales "History of local government in Wales") below it: [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Islwyn](/wiki/Islwyn "Islwyn"), [Monmouth](/wiki/District_of_Monmouth "District of Monmouth"), [Newport](/wiki/District_of_Newport "District of Newport") and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen").[[90]](#cite_note-1974_New_Wales-101) The largest five towns in the new county were [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), [Cwmbran](/wiki/Cwmbran "Cwmbran"), [Pontypool](/wiki/Pontypool "Pontypool"), [Ebbw Vale](/wiki/Ebbw_Vale "Ebbw Vale") and [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny").[[91]](#cite_note-102) In the [1979 Welsh devolution referendum](/wiki/1979_Welsh_devolution_referendum "1979 Welsh devolution referendum") Gwent voted heavily against the proposal for a Welsh Assembly with limited powers, with a "No Vote" of 87.9 per cent, with the national "No Vote" standing at 79.7 per cent.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffithsWilliamsCroll2013353-103)

### Late 20th and 21st centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Late 20th and 21st centuries")]

The [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994") created the present [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales") structure in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") of 22 [unitary authority areas](/wiki/Unitary_authorities#Wales "Unitary authorities"), the [principal areas](/wiki/Principal_area "Principal area"), and abolished the previous two-tier structure of [counties](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") and [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Wales "Districts of Wales"). It came into effect on 1 April 1996. It brought to an end the 22-year existence of Gwent, and re-created the county of Monmouthshire, although only with the eastern three-fifths of its historic area, and with a substantially reduced population. The western two-fifths of the county were included in other principal areas: [Caerphilly County Borough](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough"), part of which came from Mid Glamorgan, including the towns of [Newbridge](/wiki/Newbridge,_Caerphilly "Newbridge, Caerphilly"), [Blackwood](/wiki/Blackwood,_Caerphilly "Blackwood, Caerphilly"), [New Tredegar](/wiki/New_Tredegar "New Tredegar") and [Rhymney](/wiki/Rhymney "Rhymney"); [Blaenau Gwent County Borough](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), including [Abertillery](/wiki/Abertillery "Abertillery"), [Brynmawr](/wiki/Brynmawr "Brynmawr"), [Ebbw Vale](/wiki/Ebbw_Vale "Ebbw Vale") and [Tredegar](/wiki/Tredegar "Tredegar"); Torfaen, including [Blaenavon](/wiki/Blaenavon "Blaenavon"), [Abersychan](/wiki/Abersychan "Abersychan"), [Pontypool](/wiki/Pontypool "Pontypool"), and [Cwmbran](/wiki/Cwmbran "Cwmbran"); and the [City of Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), including [Caerleon](/wiki/Caerleon "Caerleon") as it had since 1974. The new Monmouthshire, covering the less populated eastern 60% of the historic county, included the towns of [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny"), [Caldicot](/wiki/Caldicot,_Monmouthshire "Caldicot, Monmouthshire"), [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow "Chepstow"), [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth") and [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk").[[93]](#cite_note-104) In his essay on local government in the fifth and final volume of the [Gwent County History](/wiki/Gwent_County_History "Gwent County History"), Robert McCloy suggests that the governance of "no county in the United Kingdom in the twentieth century was so transformed as that of Monmouthshire".[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloy2013126-105)

The title of Gwent continues as a [preserved county](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales"), one of eight such counties in Wales, which have mainly ceremonial functions such as the [lord lieutenancy](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Gwent "Lord Lieutenant of Gwent") and [high shrievality](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Gwent "High Sheriff of Gwent"). In 2024 the lord lieutenant is Brigadier Robert Aitken, appointed in 2016,[[95]](#cite_note-106) and the high sheriff is Professor Simon J. Gibson.[[96]](#cite_note-107) The preserved county is also retained for a limited number of public service bodies which operate across principal areas, for example [Gwent Police](/wiki/Gwent_Police "Gwent Police").[[97]](#cite_note-108) In the [1997 Welsh devolution referendum](/wiki/1997_Welsh_devolution_referendum "1997 Welsh devolution referendum") for the establishment of a [National Assembly](/wiki/Deliberative_assembly "Deliberative assembly") for Wales, which resulted in a narrow "Yes" vote, 50.30 per cent in favour v. 49.70 per cent against, Monmouthshire recorded the highest "No" vote of any principal area, its population voting 67.9 per cent against to 32.1 per cent in favour.[[98]](#cite_note-109)

Labelled relief map of the county

Monmouthshire is broadly rectangular in shape, and borders the county of [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys") to the north and the county boroughs of [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), Torfaen and [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent") to the west, with its southern border on the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary") giving the county its only coastline. To the east, it borders the English counties of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire").[[99]](#cite_note-110) The centre of the county is the plain of Gwent, formed from the basin of the [River Usk](/wiki/River_Usk "River Usk"), while the [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye") forms part of its eastern border, running through the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley"), one of the five [Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty in Wales](/wiki/Areas_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty_in_Wales "Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty in Wales") and the only one in the county.[[100]](#cite_note-111)

The north and west of the county is mountainous, particularly the western area adjoining the industrial [South Wales Valleys](/wiki/South_Wales_Valleys "South Wales Valleys") and the [Black Mountains](/wiki/Black_Mountains,_United_Kingdom "Black Mountains, United Kingdom") which form part of the [Brecon Beacons National Park](/wiki/Brecon_Beacons_National_Park "Brecon Beacons National Park"). Two major river valleys dominate the lowlands: the scenic gorge of the Wye Valley along the border with Gloucestershire adjoining the [Forest of Dean](/wiki/Forest_of_Dean "Forest of Dean"), and the valley of the River Usk between Abergavenny and Newport. Both rivers flow south to the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary"). The [River Monnow](/wiki/River_Monnow "River Monnow") is a tributary of the River Wye and forms part of the border with Herefordshire and England, passing through the town of Monmouth. The highest point of the county is [Chwarel y Fan](/wiki/Chwarel_y_Fan "Chwarel y Fan") in the Black Mountains, with a height of 679 metres (2,228 ft). The [Sugar Loaf](/wiki/Sugar_Loaf,_Monmouthshire "Sugar Loaf, Monmouthshire") (Welsh: *Mynydd Pen-y-fâl or Y Fâl*), located three kilometres (two miles) northwest of Abergavenny, offers far-reaching views; although its height is only 596 metres (1,955 ft), its isolation and distinctive peak shape make it a prominent landmark.[[101]](#cite_note-112)

[Wentwood](/wiki/Wentwood "Wentwood"), now partly in Monmouthshire and partly in [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), is the remnant of a once much larger forest, but remains the largest [ancient woodland](/wiki/Ancient_woodland "Ancient woodland") in Wales and the ninth largest in Britain.[[102]](#cite_note-113) Originally a 3,000 hectares (7,400 acres) woodland, it formed the hunting ground for Chepstow Castle, and gave its name to a traditional north–south, division of the county between the *cantrefi* (hundreds) of *Gwent Uwchcoed* (above the wood) and *Gwent Iscoed* (below the wood).[[103]](#cite_note-114)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Modern_Mons_bedrock_geology.jpg/250px-Modern_Mons_bedrock_geology.jpg)](/wiki/File:Modern_Mons_bedrock_geology.jpg)

Main article: [Geology of Monmouthshire](/wiki/Geology_of_Monmouthshire "Geology of Monmouthshire")

The centre of the principal area is dominated by the [Usk Inlier](/wiki/Usk_Inlier "Usk Inlier"), a large outcrop of [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") rock. The [inlier](/wiki/Inlier "Inlier") is surrounded by younger, [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian"), rock, including areas of [Old Red Sandstone](/wiki/Old_Red_Sandstone "Old Red Sandstone") which forms a very common building material in the county.[[104]](#cite_note-115) The [Wye Gorge](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley"), in the extreme east of the county, has a bed of [Carboniferous Limestone](/wiki/Carboniferous_Limestone "Carboniferous Limestone").[[105]](#cite_note-116)

### Coastline and landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Coastline and landscape")]

Main article: [Caldicot and Wentloog Levels](/wiki/Caldicot_and_Wentloog_Levels "Caldicot and Wentloog Levels")

Monmouth's coastline forms its southern border, running the length of the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary") from Chepstow in the east to the shore south of [Magor](/wiki/Magor,_Monmouthshire "Magor, Monmouthshire") in the west. The distance, roughly 15 miles (24 km), can be walked via the [Wales Coast Path](/wiki/Wales_Coast_Path "Wales Coast Path").[[106]](#cite_note-117) The coastline includes the eastern part of the [Caldicot and Wentloog Levels](/wiki/Caldicot_and_Wentloog_Levels "Caldicot and Wentloog Levels"), also known as the Monmouthshire or Gwent Levels, an almost entirely man-made environment that has seen [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") since [Roman times](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Roman_era "Wales in the Roman era").[[107]](#cite_note-118) The coastal area is low-lying with few cliffs, making it historically vulnerable to flooding.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans19539-119) An extreme example were the [1607 Bristol Channel floods](/wiki/1607_Bristol_Channel_floods "1607 Bristol Channel floods") which saw some 2,000 people drowned, with many victims located on Monmouthshire's coastline.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1953129-120)[[110]](#cite_note-2014bbc-121)

[Denny Island](/wiki/Denny_Island "Denny Island"), a 0.24 hectares (0.6 acres) outcrop of rock in the [Severn Estuary](/wiki/Severn_Estuary "Severn Estuary"), the southern foreshore of which is the boundary between England and Wales, is Monmouthshire's only offshore island.[[111]](#cite_note-122)

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

Main article: [List of Sites of Special Scientific Interest in Monmouthshire](/wiki/List_of_Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest_in_Monmouthshire "List of Sites of Special Scientific Interest in Monmouthshire")

The battle to save [Magor Marsh](/wiki/Magor_Marsh "Magor Marsh"), the last remaining area of natural [fenland](/wiki/Fen "Fen") on the [Gwent Levels](/wiki/Gwent_Levels "Gwent Levels"), led to the foundation of the [Gwent Wildlife Trust](/wiki/Gwent_Wildlife_Trust "Gwent Wildlife Trust").[[112]](#cite_note-123) The county contains a range of nature reserves and areas of special scientific interest, including [Graig Wood](/wiki/Graig_Wood "Graig Wood") 14.3-hectare (35-acre) SSSI, [Pentwyn Farm Grasslands](/wiki/Pentwyn_Farm_Grasslands "Pentwyn Farm Grasslands") 7.6-hectare (19-acre) SSSI and [Lady Park Wood National Nature Reserve](/wiki/Lady_Park_Wood_National_Nature_Reserve "Lady Park Wood National Nature Reserve") (45.0-hectare (111-acre)).[[113]](#cite_note-124) [Llandegfedd Reservoir](/wiki/Llandegfedd_Reservoir "Llandegfedd Reservoir"), developed in the 1960s to provide a water supply to [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), is an SSSI providing [overwintering](/wiki/Overwintering "Overwintering") for [wildfowl](/wiki/Anatidae "Anatidae").[[114]](#cite_note-125) The [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley"), the county's only [National Landscape](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty"), has its largest population of [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer") and the UK's largest population of [lesser horseshoe bats](/wiki/Lesser_horseshoe_bat "Lesser horseshoe bat").[[115]](#cite_note-126) The Wye itself was once one of the country's major centres of [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") fishing, but this has suffered very rapid decline in the 21st century due to [river pollution](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution").[[116]](#cite_note-127)[[l]](#cite_note-130)
