## Governance, politics and public services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Governance, politics and public services")]

Main article: [Monmouthshire County Council](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_Council "Monmouthshire County Council")

### Local governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Local governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Official_portrait_of_David_T._C._Davies_crop_2.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_David_T._C._Davies_crop_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_David_T._C._Davies_crop_2.jpg)

[David T. C. Davies](/wiki/David_T._C._Davies "David T. C. Davies"), [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") for [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouth (UK Parliament constituency)") 2005–2024, was the county's only member of parliament to serve as [Secretary of State for Wales](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Wales "Secretary of State for Wales")

The current [unitary authority](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Wales#Principal_areas "Subdivisions of Wales") of Monmouthshire was created on 1 April 1996 as a successor to the [district of Monmouth](/wiki/District_of_Monmouth "District of Monmouth") along with the [Llanelly](/wiki/Llanelly "Llanelly") community from Blaenau Gwent, both of which were [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Wales "Districts of Wales") of Gwent. It is a [principal area](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") of Wales.[[m]](#cite_note-133) Monmouthshire is styled as a county, and includes: the former boroughs of Abergavenny and Monmouth; the former urban districts of [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow "Chepstow") and Usk; the former rural districts of Abergavenny, Chepstow and Monmouth; the former rural district of [Pontypool](/wiki/Pontypool "Pontypool"), except the community of [Llanfrechfa Lower](/wiki/Llanfrechfa_Lower "Llanfrechfa Lower"); and the parish of [Llanelly](/wiki/Llanelly "Llanelly") from the former [Crickhowell](/wiki/Crickhowell "Crickhowell") Rural District in Brecknockshire.[[121]](#cite_note-134)

The county is administered by [Monmouthshire County Council](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_Council "Monmouthshire County Council"), with its head office at Rhadyr, outside [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk"), opened in 2013.[[122]](#cite_note-135)[[123]](#cite_note-136)[[124]](#cite_note-137) In the [2022 Monmouthshire County Council election](/wiki/2022_Monmouthshire_County_Council_election "2022 Monmouthshire County Council election"), no party gained overall control, with the [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") party forming a minority administration, its 22 councillors allying with five [Independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") and one [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") councillor. The council leader is Mary Ann Brocklesby.[[125]](#cite_note-138)

### National representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: National representation")]

Monmouthshire elects one [member](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") to [the UK parliament at Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), until 2024 representing the [Monmouth constituency](/wiki/Monmouth_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouth (UK Parliament constituency)"). Under the [2023 Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_Periodic_Review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies"), a new constituency, [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouthshire (UK Parliament constituency)"), came into effect at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), comprising 88.9% of the previous constituency.[[126]](#cite_note-139) The seat was won by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(United_Kingdom) "Labour Party (United Kingdom)") candidate [Catherine Fookes](/wiki/Catherine_Fookes "Catherine Fookes")[[127]](#cite_note-140) who defeated the incumbent, [David T. C. Davies](/wiki/David_T._C._Davies "David T. C. Davies"), a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") politician who had held the previous seat since 2005 and who served as the [Secretary of State for Wales](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Wales "Secretary of State for Wales") in the prior government.[[128]](#cite_note-141)

Monmouthshire directly elects two members to the [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd"), the Welsh parliament. The [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_(Senedd_constituency) "Monmouth (Senedd constituency)") constituency covers most of the county and since May 2021 the directly elected member is [Peter Fox](/wiki/Peter_Fox_(Welsh_politician) "Peter Fox (Welsh politician)"),[[129]](#cite_note-Senedd_2021_result-142) a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") politician who previously served as the chair of Monmouthshire County Council.[[130]](#cite_note-143) The western edge of the county, bordering Newport and including the settlements of [Magor](/wiki/Magor,_Monmouthshire "Magor, Monmouthshire"), [Undy](/wiki/Undy "Undy"), [Rogiet](/wiki/Rogiet "Rogiet") and [Caldicot](/wiki/Caldicot "Caldicot"), forms part of the [Newport East](/wiki/Newport_East_(Senedd_constituency) "Newport East (Senedd constituency)") constituency which has [John Griffiths](/wiki/John_Griffiths_(Welsh_politician) "John Griffiths (Welsh politician)") of [Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") as its member.[[131]](#cite_note-144)

Monmouth is also one of eight constituencies in the [South Wales East](/wiki/South_Wales_East_(Senedd_electoral_region) "South Wales East (Senedd electoral region)") [electoral region](/wiki/Senedd_constituencies_and_electoral_regions "Senedd constituencies and electoral regions"), which elects four [additional members](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system"), under a partial proportional representation system.[[132]](#cite_note-145)

### Public services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Public services")]

Fire and rescue services are provided by [South Wales Fire and Rescue Service](/wiki/South_Wales_Fire_and_Rescue_Service "South Wales Fire and Rescue Service"), which has fire stations in the county at Abergavenny, Caldicot, Chepstow, Monmouth and Usk.[[133]](#cite_note-146) Policing services are provided by [Gwent Police](/wiki/Gwent_Police "Gwent Police"), whose officers cover Monmouthshire, as well as [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") and Torfaen.[[134]](#cite_note-147) Civilian oversight is provided by the [Gwent Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/Gwent_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "Gwent Police and Crime Commissioner").[[135]](#cite_note-148) Monmouthshire's prisons are [HM Prison Prescoed](/wiki/HM_Prison_Prescoed "HM Prison Prescoed"), a [Category D open prison](/wiki/Prisoner_security_categories_in_the_United_Kingdom "Prisoner security categories in the United Kingdom") at [Coed-y-paen](/wiki/Coed-y-paen "Coed-y-paen") and [HM Prison Usk](/wiki/HM_Prison_Usk "HM Prison Usk"), a Category C prison, both in the west of the county.[[136]](#cite_note-149)

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Demography")]

[![Graphic showing the proportion of Welsh speakers throughout Wales in 2011](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png/250px-Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)](/wiki/File:Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)

Proportion of Welsh speakers (Wales 2011 census)

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Population")]

Monmouthshire's population was 93,000 at the 2021 census, increasing marginally from 91,300 at the 2011 census. 54,100 (58.2 per cent) of residents were born in Wales, while 32,300 (34.7 per cent) were born in England.[[137]](#cite_note-Census2021-150) Just over 20 per cent of the county's population is over the age of 65. It remains one of the least densely populated of Wales's [principal areas](/wiki/Principal_area "Principal area").[[138]](#cite_note-auto5-151)

### Language, ethnicity and identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Language, ethnicity and identity")]

The 2021 census recorded that [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") is spoken by 8.7 per cent of the population of the county, a decrease from 9.9 per cent in 2011. The number of non-Welsh speakers increased by 3,000 over the decade.[[137]](#cite_note-Census2021-150) In 2021, 96.9 per cent of Monmouthshire residents identified as "white European", marginally lower than in 2011, compared with 98 per cent for the whole of Wales.[[137]](#cite_note-Census2021-150) 41.9 per cent of the population identified as "Welsh", down from 44.0% in 2011. The percentage of residents in Monmouthshire that identified as "British only" increased from 23.5% to 27.0%.[[137]](#cite_note-Census2021-150)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Religion")]

In the 2021 census 43.4 per cent of Monmouthshire residents reported having "No religion", an increase of nearly 15 per cent from the 28.5 per cent in the 2011 census. 48.7 per cent described themselves as "Christian" with the remainder reporting themselves as Buddhist (0.4 percent); Hindu (0.2 per cent); Jewish (0.1 per cent); Muslim (0.5 per cent); Sikh (0.1 per cent) or Other (0.6 per cent).[[137]](#cite_note-Census2021-150) The [Diocese of Monmouth](/wiki/Diocese_of_Monmouth "Diocese of Monmouth"), within the [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales"), serves the area covered by the historic county, and its [bishop](/wiki/Bishop_of_Monmouth "Bishop of Monmouth"), from 2019 [Cherry Vann](/wiki/Cherry_Vann "Cherry Vann"), is based at Newport.[[139]](#cite_note-auto-152)[[n]](#cite_note-153)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Employment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Sheep_Shearing.jpg/250px-Sheep_Shearing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheep_Shearing.jpg)

[Sheep shearing](/wiki/Sheep_shearing "Sheep shearing") at the [Monmouthshire Show](/wiki/Monmouthshire_Show "Monmouthshire Show"): agriculture remains an important employer in the county

Monmouthshire is now primarily a [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy"), with professional, scientific and technical businesses, financial services, IT and business administration, retail, hospitality and arts and entertainment businesses accounting for just over 50 per cent of the total number of enterprises in the county. Firms are generally small, with 91 per cent of businesses employing fewer than 10 people.[[140]](#cite_note-auto3-154) It is a relatively prosperous county in comparison with the average in Wales; 80.0 per cent of people of working age are in employment compared with the Welsh average of 72.8 per cent; just under 3,000 people were in receipt of the main unemployment benefit, a substantially lower number than in all of the adjoining principal areas; average annual earnings in 2020 were just over £41,000 compared to just over £32,000 in Wales as a whole. Total [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") payments from the county in 2013 were second only to the City of Cardiff, and the average individual payment exceeded that paid in the capital city.[[141]](#cite_note-155) Agriculture continues to be an important employer, accounting for 15.3 per cent of businesses, the second largest single sector after professional, scientific and technical enterprises. The [Monmouthshire Show](/wiki/Monmouthshire_Show "Monmouthshire Show"), an annual [agricultural show](/wiki/Agricultural_show "Agricultural show"), is one of the largest such events in Wales and has operated since 1790.[[142]](#cite_note-156) The third largest individual employment sector is construction.[[140]](#cite_note-auto3-154)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Transport")]

#### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Road")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/New_severn_bridge_best_750pix.jpg/250px-New_severn_bridge_best_750pix.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_severn_bridge_best_750pix.jpg)

[Second Severn Crossing](/wiki/Second_Severn_Crossing "Second Severn Crossing") – opened in 1996 and now the Prince of Wales Bridge

The only [motorways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") are in the south of the county: the [M4](/wiki/M4_motorway "M4 motorway") which connects Wales with England via the [Prince of Wales Bridge](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Bridge "Prince of Wales Bridge") with its Welsh end near [Sudbrook](/wiki/Sudbrook,_Monmouthshire "Sudbrook, Monmouthshire"); and the [M48](/wiki/M48_motorway "M48 motorway"), originally part of the M4,[[143]](#cite_note-157) which links Wales with England via the [Severn Bridge](/wiki/Severn_Bridge "Severn Bridge") at Chepstow.[[144]](#cite_note-158) In the east of the county, the [A449](/wiki/A449_road "A449 road") and the [A40](/wiki/A40_road "A40 road") link with the [M50](/wiki/M50_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M50 motorway (Great Britain)") near [Goodrich, Herefordshire](/wiki/Goodrich,_Herefordshire "Goodrich, Herefordshire"), connecting Monmouthshire and South Wales with the [English Midlands](/wiki/English_Midlands "English Midlands").[[145]](#cite_note-159) The conversion of the [A465 road](/wiki/A465_road "A465 road") from Abergavenny to [Dowlais](/wiki/Dowlais "Dowlais") into a [dual carriageway](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway") was completed in the mid-2020s, after being proposed in the 1990s. Despite major cost overruns,[[146]](#cite_note-160) it is considered to have brought benefits to the area.[[147]](#cite_note-161) The [Department for Transport](/wiki/Department_for_Transport "Department for Transport") recorded traffic in Monmouthshire at 0.9 billion [vehicle miles](/wiki/Units_of_measurement_in_transportation#Traffic_flow "Units of measurement in transportation") in 2022. This represented a lower level of road usage than in 2016.[[148]](#cite_note-auto2-162)[[o]](#cite_note-163)

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Rail")]

Monmouthshire is served by four railway stations: in the south are the [Severn Tunnel Junction railway station](/wiki/Severn_Tunnel_Junction_railway_station "Severn Tunnel Junction railway station") at [Rogiet](/wiki/Rogiet "Rogiet") on the [South Wales Main Line](/wiki/South_Wales_Main_Line "South Wales Main Line"), which connects South Wales to London; and [Chepstow railway station](/wiki/Chepstow_railway_station "Chepstow railway station") and [Caldicot railway station](/wiki/Caldicot_railway_station "Caldicot railway station") on the [Gloucester–Newport line](/wiki/Gloucester%E2%80%93Newport_line "Gloucester–Newport line"); and in the north, [Abergavenny railway station](/wiki/Abergavenny_railway_station "Abergavenny railway station") on the [Welsh Marches line](/wiki/Welsh_Marches_line "Welsh Marches line").[[149]](#cite_note-164)

#### Bus services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Bus services")]

The county's main centres of population are served by a bus network, connecting Abergavenny, Monmouth, Chepstow, Raglan and Usk, with stopping points at smaller settlements on route.[[150]](#cite_note-165) National coach services have stopping points at Monmouth and Chepstow.[[151]](#cite_note-166)[[152]](#cite_note-167)

#### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Waterways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Tintern_Abbey_and_Courtyard.jpg/250px-Tintern_Abbey_and_Courtyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tintern_Abbey_and_Courtyard.jpg)

[Tintern Abbey](/wiki/Tintern_Abbey "Tintern Abbey"), the highlight of the [Wye Tour](/wiki/Wye_Tour "Wye Tour")

In its industrial heyday in the 18th and 19th centuries, the eastern periphery of the [South Wales Coalfield](/wiki/South_Wales_Coalfield "South Wales Coalfield") was served by the [Monmouthshire and Brecon Canal](/wiki/Monmouthshire_and_Brecon_Canal "Monmouthshire and Brecon Canal") which connected the coalfield with the port at [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"). Today, the canal is a popular route for leisure cruising but most of its length lies within the principal areas of Torfaen, [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent") and Newport.[[153]](#cite_note-168) The Monmouthshire villages of [Gilwern](/wiki/Gilwern "Gilwern"), [Govilon](/wiki/Govilon "Govilon") and [Goetre](/wiki/Goetre "Goetre"), on the western extremity of the county, remain adjacent to the canal.[[154]](#cite_note-169)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Tourism remains an important element of the county's economy. It generated just under £245 million in income in 2019, from 2.28 million visitors. The sector also provides employment for over 3,000 inhabitants of the county,[[140]](#cite_note-auto3-154) approximately 10 per cent of the total working population.[[138]](#cite_note-auto5-151)

## Education and health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Education and health")]

See also: [List of schools in Monmouthshire](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Monmouthshire "List of schools in Monmouthshire")

### Higher, further, secondary, primary and special education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Higher, further, secondary, primary and special education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Eastern_side_of_King_Henry_VIII_School%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2745528.jpg/250px-Eastern_side_of_King_Henry_VIII_School%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2745528.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastern_side_of_King_Henry_VIII_School,_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2745528.jpg)

[King Henry VIII School, Abergavenny](/wiki/King_Henry_VIII_School,_Abergavenny "King Henry VIII School, Abergavenny")

The county has neither a university nor any satellite campus.[[155]](#cite_note-170)[[p]](#cite_note-172) The former [University of Wales, Newport](/wiki/University_of_Wales,_Newport "University of Wales, Newport") operated a [campus](/wiki/Caerleon_campus "Caerleon campus") at [Caerleon](/wiki/University_of_South_Wales#Former_campuses "University of South Wales") which closed in 2016, following the 2013 merger which created the [University of South Wales](/wiki/University_of_South_Wales "University of South Wales").[[157]](#cite_note-173) Higher education courses in the county are provided through the campus of [Coleg Gwent](/wiki/Coleg_Gwent "Coleg Gwent") at Rhadyr, near [Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk").[[158]](#cite_note-174)

There are four [maintained secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Wales "Secondary education in Wales") in the county,[[159]](#cite_note-auto1-175) [Caldicot School](/wiki/Caldicot_School "Caldicot School"), serving the south of the county; [Monmouth Comprehensive School](/wiki/Monmouth_Comprehensive_School "Monmouth Comprehensive School") serving the east; [Chepstow School](/wiki/Chepstow_School "Chepstow School"), serving the town of [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow "Chepstow") and the surrounding villages; and [King Henry VIII 3–19 School](/wiki/King_Henry_VIII_3%E2%80%9319_School,_Abergavenny "King Henry VIII 3–19 School, Abergavenny") in [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny"), serving the town and the north of the county. All have [sixth-forms](/wiki/Sixth-form "Sixth-form").[[160]](#cite_note-176) There was one special school, Mounton House School, based at [Mounton House](/wiki/Mounton_House "Mounton House") near Chepstow, but that closed in 2020 and, as at 2024, there is no specific special school provision.[[161]](#cite_note-177) There are 30 [primary schools](/wiki/Primary_education_in_Wales "Primary education in Wales") of which two are [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") medium. There are no full Welsh language medium secondary schools, although all offer the option of studying Welsh.[[159]](#cite_note-auto1-175) The only [independent secondary provision](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom") in the county is [Haberdashers' Monmouth School](/wiki/Haberdashers%27_Monmouth_School "Haberdashers' Monmouth School"),[[162]](#cite_note-178)[[163]](#cite_note-179) formed in 2024 from an amalgamation of [Monmouth School for Boys](/wiki/Monmouth_School_for_Boys "Monmouth School for Boys") and [Monmouth School for Girls](/wiki/Monmouth_School_for_Girls "Monmouth School for Girls"), and operated by the [Haberdashers' Company](/wiki/Worshipful_Company_of_Haberdashers "Worshipful Company of Haberdashers").[[164]](#cite_note-180)

### Health services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Health services")]

The [Aneurin Bevan University Health Board](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan_University_Health_Board "Aneurin Bevan University Health Board") is the [Local health board](/wiki/Local_health_board "Local health board") for Gwent within [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales") and has responsibility for health care within the county.[[165]](#cite_note-181) The largest hospital in the county is the [Nevill Hall Hospital](/wiki/Nevill_Hall_Hospital "Nevill Hall Hospital") at Abergavenny. Its range of services has reduced following the opening of the specialist critical care centre at the [Grange University Hospital](/wiki/Grange_University_Hospital "Grange University Hospital") in Torfaen in 2020. The Grange is also the designated trauma centre for Gwent, which covers Monmouthshire.[[166]](#cite_note-182) The [Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015](/wiki/Well-being_of_Future_Generations_(Wales)_Act_2015 "Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015") established Public Services Boards throughout Wales to oversee health and well-being, and following reorganisation in 2021 a Gwent public services board was created to have oversight for Monmouthshire, Blaenau Gwent, Caerphilly, Newport and Torfaen.[[167]](#cite_note-183)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Flag_of_Monmouthshire.svg/250px-Flag_of_Monmouthshire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Monmouthshire.svg)

Monmouthshire's flag, adopted in 2011

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Flag")]

The [flag of Monmouthshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Monmouthshire "Flag of Monmouthshire") was officially adopted in 2011.[[168]](#cite_note-184) It features three gold [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") on a black/blue background.[[169]](#cite_note-185)

### Built and landscape heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Built and landscape heritage")]

Main articles: [Grade I listed buildings in Monmouthshire](/wiki/Grade_I_listed_buildings_in_Monmouthshire "Grade I listed buildings in Monmouthshire"), [Grade II\* listed buildings in Monmouthshire](/wiki/Grade_II*_listed_buildings_in_Monmouthshire "Grade II* listed buildings in Monmouthshire"), and [Registered historic parks and gardens in Monmouthshire](/wiki/Registered_historic_parks_and_gardens_in_Monmouthshire "Registered historic parks and gardens in Monmouthshire")

Monmouthshire has 2,428 [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_buildings "Listed buildings"),[[170]](#cite_note-186) including 54 at Grade I,[[171]](#cite_note-187) the highest grade, and 246 at Grade II\*, the next highest grade.[[172]](#cite_note-188) These include churches, a priory and an abbey, and several castles. The journalist [Simon Jenkins](/wiki/Simon_Jenkins "Simon Jenkins") notes the county's "fine collection" of these,[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins2008163-189) mostly dating from the [Norman invasion of Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales"), and describes [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow_Castle "Chepstow Castle") as "the glory of medieval south Wales".[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins2008169-190) The castle at [Raglan](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle") is later, dating from the mid-fifteenth century.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins2008174-191) The fortified [bridge](/wiki/Monnow_Bridge "Monnow Bridge") over the [River Monnow](/wiki/River_Monnow "River Monnow") at [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth") is the only remaining fortified river bridge in the country with its gate tower standing on the bridge, and has been described as "arguably the finest surviving medieval bridge in Britain".[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayman201669-192) Monmouthshire has a more "modest"[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins2008163-189) range of churches, although that at [Bettws Newydd](/wiki/Church_of_St_Aeddan,_Bettws_Newydd "Church of St Aeddan, Bettws Newydd") has "perhaps the most complete [rood](/wiki/Rood_screen "Rood screen") arrangement remaining in any church in [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")".[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2000120-193) The county's Grade I listed abbey, at [Tintern](/wiki/Tintern_Abbey "Tintern Abbey"), became a focal point of the [Wye Tour](/wiki/Wye_Tour "Wye Tour")[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETyermanWarner19513-194) in the late-eighteenth century.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell201065–74-195) The county has 48 [registered historic landscapes](/wiki/Cadw/ICOMOS_Register_of_Parks_and_Gardens_of_Special_Historic_Interest_in_Wales "Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales") including five at Grade I and nine at Grade II\*.[[180]](#cite_note-196)

### Sport and leisure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Sport and leisure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Monmouth_rowing_club.jpg/250px-Monmouth_rowing_club.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monmouth_rowing_club.jpg)

[Monmouth Rowing Club](/wiki/Monmouth_Rowing_Club "Monmouth Rowing Club") boathouse on the [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye")

Monmouthshire has [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") clubs at [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny_RFC "Abergavenny RFC") and [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_RFC "Monmouth RFC"),[[181]](#cite_note-197)[[182]](#cite_note-198) and an invitational county team, [Monmouthshire County RFC](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_RFC "Monmouthshire County RFC"). It has [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") clubs at [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny_Town_F.C. "Abergavenny Town F.C."),[[183]](#cite_note-199) [Caldicot](/wiki/Caldicot_Town_F.C. "Caldicot Town F.C."),[[184]](#cite_note-200) [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow_Town_F.C. "Chepstow Town F.C.")[[185]](#cite_note-201) and [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_Town_F.C. "Monmouth Town F.C.").[[186]](#cite_note-202) The football clubs play in the [Ardal Leagues](/wiki/Ardal_Leagues "Ardal Leagues")[[187]](#cite_note-203) and the [Gwent County League](/wiki/Gwent_County_League "Gwent County League").[[188]](#cite_note-204) [Monmouthshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_Cricket_Club "Monmouthshire County Cricket Club") was established in the 19th century and achieved a notable victory in 1858 when a Monmouthshire XXII beat an [England XI](/wiki/Rest_of_England_cricket_teams "Rest of England cricket teams") at a match on Newport Marshes. The club suffered financial difficulties in the 1930s and merged with [Glamorgan County Cricket Club](/wiki/Glamorgan_County_Cricket_Club "Glamorgan County Cricket Club") in 1934.[[189]](#cite_note-205) Monmouthshire has a rowing tradition on the River Wye, with the [Monmouth Rowing Club](/wiki/Monmouth_Rowing_Club "Monmouth Rowing Club"), founded in 1928,[[190]](#cite_note-206) and all three of the town's secondary schools having their own rowing clubs.[[191]](#cite_note-207)[[192]](#cite_note-208)[[193]](#cite_note-209)

[Chepstow Racecourse](/wiki/Chepstow_Racecourse "Chepstow Racecourse") hosts the [Coral Welsh Grand National](/wiki/Welsh_Grand_National "Welsh Grand National"), the richest [thoroughbred horse racing](/wiki/Thoroughbred_horse_racing "Thoroughbred horse racing") event in Wales.[[194]](#cite_note-210) [The Rolls of Monmouth Golf Club](/wiki/The_Hendre "The Hendre") at [Rockfield](/wiki/Rockfield,_Monmouthshire "Rockfield, Monmouthshire") is ranked in the 50 top courses in Wales,[[195]](#cite_note-211) while the [St Pierre course](/wiki/St_Pierre,_Monmouthshire#Golf_club "St Pierre, Monmouthshire") in the south of the county hosted the [Epson Grand Prix of Europe](/wiki/Epson_Grand_Prix_of_Europe "Epson Grand Prix of Europe") and the [British Masters](/wiki/British_Masters "British Masters") in the late 20th century.[[196]](#cite_note-212)

Monmouthshire has some major [caving](/wiki/Caving "Caving") sites. [Ogof Draenen](/wiki/Ogof_Draenen "Ogof Draenen"), at [Pwll Du](/wiki/Pwll_Du "Pwll Du") in the north west of the county, is the longest [cave](/wiki/Cave "Cave") system in Wales, and the second-longest in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain").[[197]](#cite_note-213) Its full extent was identified by members of the Morgannwg Caving Club in 1994.[[198]](#cite_note-214) In the south of the county, underneath Chepstow Racecourse, [Otter Hole](/wiki/Otter_Hole "Otter Hole") is considered one of the best [decorated](/wiki/Speleothem "Speleothem") caves in the country.[[199]](#cite_note-215)

Several [long-distance footpaths](/wiki/Long-distance_trail "Long-distance trail") pass through the county, including the [Marches Way](/wiki/Marches_Way "Marches Way"), the [Three Castles Walk](/wiki/Three_Castles_Walk,_Monmouthshire "Three Castles Walk, Monmouthshire"), [Offa's Dyke Path](/wiki/Offa%27s_Dyke_Path "Offa's Dyke Path"), the [Usk Valley Walk](/wiki/Usk_Valley_Walk "Usk Valley Walk"), the [Monnow Valley Walk](/wiki/Monnow_Valley_Walk "Monnow Valley Walk") and the [Wye Valley Walk](/wiki/Wye_Valley_Walk "Wye Valley Walk").[[200]](#cite_note-216) Chepstow is a terminus for two long-distance cycle routes which form part of the [National Cycle Network](/wiki/National_Cycle_Network "National Cycle Network"): [National Cycle Route 8](/wiki/National_Cycle_Route_8 "National Cycle Route 8") which runs from either Chepstow or Cardiff in the south to [Holyhead](/wiki/Holyhead "Holyhead") in the north, and the [Celtic Trail cycle route](/wiki/Celtic_Trail_cycle_route "Celtic Trail cycle route") which runs east to west, from Chepstow to [Fishguard](/wiki/Fishguard "Fishguard").[[201]](#cite_note-217)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Augusta_Hall.jpg/250px-Augusta_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Augusta_Hall.jpg)

[Lady Llanover](/wiki/Augusta_Hall,_Baroness_Llanover "Augusta Hall, Baroness Llanover"), 1862

Main article: [Cuisine of Monmouthshire](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Monmouthshire "Cuisine of Monmouthshire")

The cuisine of Monmouthshire traditionally focused on its local produce, including [lamb and mutton](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") from [sheep farming](/wiki/Sheep_farming_in_Wales "Sheep farming in Wales") in the hillier north of the county,[[202]](#cite_note-218) poultry and [game](/wiki/Game_(hunting) "Game (hunting)").[[203]](#cite_note-219) [Lady Llanover](/wiki/Augusta_Hall,_Baroness_Llanover "Augusta Hall, Baroness Llanover"), (bardic name *Gwenynen Gwent* — "the bee of Gwent"), was an early champion of Welsh culture and cuisine; her *First Principles of Good Cookery*, published in 1867, was one of the first Welsh cookery books.[[204]](#cite_note-220) The contemporary writer, Gilli Davies, in her study of Welsh food, *Tastes of Wales*, writes of the "rare and appealing quality to the food in Monmouthshire".[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies199070-221) The county has a small [viniculture](/wiki/Viniculture "Viniculture") industry, with vineyards at [Ancre Hill Estates](/wiki/Ancre_Hill_Estates "Ancre Hill Estates"), north of Monmouth; [White Castle](/wiki/White_Castle,_Monmouthshire "White Castle, Monmouthshire") vineyard near Abergavenny,[[206]](#cite_note-222) and the [Tintern Parva](/wiki/Tintern "Tintern") vineyard in the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley").[[207]](#cite_note-223)[[208]](#cite_note-224) There are two [Michelin starred](/wiki/Michelin_Guide#Stars "Michelin Guide") restaurants in Monmouthshire, [The Walnut Tree](/wiki/The_Walnut_Tree_(restaurant) "The Walnut Tree (restaurant)") at [Llanddewi Skirrid](/wiki/Llanddewi_Skirrid "Llanddewi Skirrid"),[[209]](#cite_note-225) in the north of the county and [The Whitebrook](/wiki/The_Whitebrook "The Whitebrook") at [Whitebrook](/wiki/Whitebrook "Whitebrook") in the east.[[210]](#cite_note-226)[[211]](#cite_note-227) [Abergavenny Food Festival](/wiki/Abergavenny_Food_Festival "Abergavenny Food Festival") is held annually each September. Established in 1991, it has been described as one of Britain's best food and produce events.[[212]](#cite_note-228)[[213]](#cite_note-229)[[214]](#cite_note-230)

### Media, the arts and local history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media, the arts and local history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/The_Savoy_Theatre%2C_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1070497.jpg/250px-The_Savoy_Theatre%2C_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1070497.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Savoy_Theatre,_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1070497.jpg)

The [Savoy Theatre, Monmouth](/wiki/Savoy_Theatre,_Monmouth "Savoy Theatre, Monmouth") claims to be Wales's oldest

Monmouthshire has three local newspapers, the *Abergavenny Chronicle*, the *Forest of Dean and Wye Valley Review* and the *[Monmouthshire Beacon](/wiki/Monmouthshire_Beacon "Monmouthshire Beacon")*. All are published by [Tindle](/wiki/Tindle "Tindle"), a regional media group.[[215]](#cite_note-231) Digital reporting is provided by the Monmouthshire Free Press Series.[[216]](#cite_note-232) [Sunshine Radio (Herefordshire and Monmouthshire)](/wiki/Sunshine_Radio_(Herefordshire_and_Monmouthshire) "Sunshine Radio (Herefordshire and Monmouthshire)") is the only local radio station, although it is based in Hereford.[[217]](#cite_note-233) [Rockfield Studios](/wiki/Rockfield_Studios "Rockfield Studios") is a major residential [recording studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio") which has seen bands and artists such as [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") and the [Manic Street Preachers](/wiki/Manic_Street_Preachers "Manic Street Preachers") record material. [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") recorded most of "[Bohemian Rhapsody](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody "Bohemian Rhapsody")" at Rockfield in 1975.[[218]](#cite_note-234)

There are two theatres in Monmouthshire, the [Borough Theatre](/wiki/Borough_Theatre "Borough Theatre") in Abergavenny,[[219]](#cite_note-235) and the [Savoy Theatre, Monmouth](/wiki/Savoy_Theatre,_Monmouth "Savoy Theatre, Monmouth"). Operated by a charitable trust, the Savoy claims to be the oldest theatre in Wales.[[220]](#cite_note-freepress-236) Museums of local life are located at [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny_Museum "Abergavenny Museum"),[[221]](#cite_note-237) [Chepstow](/wiki/Chepstow_Museum "Chepstow Museum"),[[222]](#cite_note-238) [Usk](/wiki/Usk#Usk_Rural_Life_Museum "Usk")[[223]](#cite_note-239) and [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_Museum "Monmouth Museum"). During the closure of the Monmouth museum in 2020–2021 in the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), the council announced that the museum would not re-open and that its collections, including an important assemblage of memorabilia related to [Admiral Lord Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson") donated to the town by [Georgiana, Lady Llangattock](/wiki/Georgiana_Rolls,_Baroness_Llangattock "Georgiana Rolls, Baroness Llangattock"),[[224]](#cite_note-240) would be relocated to the [Shire Hall](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Monmouth "Shire Hall, Monmouth"). The museum's [Market Hall](/wiki/Market_Hall,_Monmouth "Market Hall, Monmouth") site would be redeveloped for commercial use. The council intends to complete the transfer by 2027.[[225]](#cite_note-241) The [Monmouth Regimental Museum](/wiki/Monmouth_Regimental_Museum "Monmouth Regimental Museum"), located at [Great Castle House](/wiki/Great_Castle_House "Great Castle House") in Monmouth, contains material related to the [Royal Monmouthshire Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Monmouthshire_Royal_Engineers "Royal Monmouthshire Royal Engineers").[[226]](#cite_note-242)

In 2012, the launch of [Monmouthpedia](/wiki/Monmouthpedia "Monmouthpedia"), a project to use [QRpedia](/wiki/QRpedia "QRpedia") [QR codes](/wiki/QR_codes "QR codes") to provide multilingual [smart phone](/wiki/Smart_phone "Smart phone") access to Wikipedia articles on notable subjects in the area, saw Monmouth described as the "world's first Wikipedia town".[[227]](#cite_note-243)

## Historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Historiography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Dixton_Church%2C_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_604133.jpg/250px-Dixton_Church%2C_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_604133.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dixton_Church,_Monmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_604133.jpg)

"The lovely white church of [Dixton](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Church,_Dixton "St Peter's Church, Dixton")"[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHando1958115–117-244) – [Fred Hando](/wiki/Fred_Hando "Fred Hando") chronicled many such "little places of a shy county".[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHando194415-245)

The development of tourism in the late 18th century saw the writing of a number of histories of the area, which frequently combined the features of a guidebook with a more formal historical approach. Among the first was [William Gilpin](/wiki/William_Gilpin_(priest) "William Gilpin (priest)")'s *Observations*, published in 1782.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilpin1782-246) Among the most notable was [William Coxe](/wiki/William_Coxe_(historian) "William Coxe (historian)")'s two-volume *An Historical Tour in Monmouthshire*, published in 1801. Coxe's preface explains the tour's genesis: "The present work owes its origin to an accidental excursion in Monmouthshire, in company with my friend [Sir Richard Hoare](/wiki/Sir_Richard_Hoare,_2nd_Baronet "Sir Richard Hoare, 2nd Baronet"), during the autumn of 1798."[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoxe1995aPreface-247) A detailed county history was undertaken by [Sir Joseph Bradney](/wiki/Joseph_Bradney "Joseph Bradney"), in his *[A History of Monmouthshire from the Coming of the Normans into Wales down to the Present Time](/wiki/A_History_of_Monmouthshire_from_the_Coming_of_the_Normans_into_Wales_down_to_the_Present_Time "A History of Monmouthshire from the Coming of the Normans into Wales down to the Present Time")*, published over a period of 30 years in the early 20th century.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradney1991preface-248)

Studies of the architecture of the county include [John Newman](/wiki/John_Newman_(architectural_historian) "John Newman (architectural historian)")'s *Gwent/Monmouthshire* volume of the [Pevsner Buildings of Wales series](/wiki/Pevsner_Architectural_Guides "Pevsner Architectural Guides"); and, most exhaustively, [Sir Cyril Fox](/wiki/Cyril_Fox "Cyril Fox") and [Lord Raglan](/wiki/FitzRoy_Somerset,_4th_Baron_Raglan "FitzRoy Somerset, 4th Baron Raglan")'s three-volume study, *[Monmouthshire Houses](/wiki/Monmouthshire_Houses "Monmouthshire Houses")*.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoxRaglan1994preface-249) This was described by the architectural historian [Peter Smith](/wiki/Peter_Smith_(architectural_historian) "Peter Smith (architectural historian)"), author of the magisterial *Houses of the Welsh Countryside*, as "one of the most remarkable studies of vernacular architecture yet made in the British Isles,[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith19757-250) a landmark, in its own field, as significant as [Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s *[Origin of Species](/wiki/Origin_of_Species "Origin of Species")*".[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200084-251)

The 20th century saw the publication of two lesser histories: [Hugo Tyerman](/wiki/Hugo_Tyerman "Hugo Tyerman") and Sydney Warner's *Monmouthshire* volume of [Arthur Mee](/wiki/Arthur_Mee "Arthur Mee")'s *[The King's England](/wiki/The_King%27s_England "The King's England")* series in 1951;[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETyermanWarner19513-194) and Arthur Clark's two-volume *The Story of Monmouthshire*, published in 1979–1980.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1979Introduction-252)[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1980Introduction-253) The history of the county was covered in more anecdotal form by the Monmouthshire writer and artist [Fred Hando](/wiki/Fred_Hando "Fred Hando"), who chronicled the highways and byways of the county in some 800 newspaper articles written from the 1920s until his death in 1970 and published in the [South Wales Argus](/wiki/South_Wales_Argus "South Wales Argus"), focusing on "the little places of a shy county".[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHando194415-245) The 21st century saw the publication of the county's most important history, the five-volume *[Gwent County History](/wiki/Gwent_County_History "Gwent County History")*. The series, modelled on the [Victoria County History](/wiki/Victoria_County_History "Victoria County History") and with [Ralph A. Griffiths](/wiki/Ralph_A._Griffiths "Ralph A. Griffiths") as editor-in-chief, was published by the [University of Wales Press](/wiki/University_of_Wales_Press "University of Wales Press") between 2004 and 2013 and covered the history of the county from prehistoric times to the 21st century.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen2004-254)[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffithsWilliamsCroll2013-255)

### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monmouthshire&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") footprints, dated to about 8,000 years ago, have been uncovered on the [foreshore](/wiki/Foreshore "Foreshore") of the Severn Estuary at [Goldcliff](/wiki/Goldcliff "Goldcliff"), formerly in Monmouthshire but now in Newport.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Much the most important Roman site in the area is [Isca Augusta](/wiki/Isca_Augusta "Isca Augusta"), at [Caerleon](/wiki/Caerleon "Caerleon"), founded as the headquarters of the [Augustan Second Legion](/wiki/Legio_II_Augusta "Legio II Augusta") in around AD 75. The site was historically in Monmouthshire, but is now part of [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales").[[16]](#cite_note-17)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** Modern scholarship suggests a greater role for migration, co-existence, and inter-marriage between the incoming Anglos-Saxons and the native inhabitants, and a lesser role for invasion and combat, as recounted by chroniclers from [Gildas](/wiki/Gildas "Gildas") onwards.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHärke20111–28-22)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** Raymond Howell, in his county history published in 1988, notes the significance of the retention by the Kingdom of Gwent of both banks of the lower [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye") at the time of [Offa](/wiki/Offa "Offa")'s construction work, indicating their ability to treat almost as equals with the most powerful of the Saxon kingdoms.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell198845–46-26)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-35)** John Kenyon noted that [Chepstow Castle](/wiki/Chepstow_Castle "Chepstow Castle") is among the best researched in the British Isles.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon2010113-34)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-38)** Howell writes, "as literature, Geoffrey's work was a classic, as history it was virtually useless. Nevertheless, because of wide-spread influence, the myths of Geoffrey became institutionalized as history".[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowell1988204–205-37) Neil Wright is equally clear, "the Historia does not bear scrutiny as an authentic history and no scholar today would regard it as such".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright1984xxviii-36)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** The castle had fallen into ruin by the 17th century; on the occasion of his visit in 1798, [Archdeacon Coxe](/wiki/William_Coxe_(historian) "William Coxe (historian)") described it as presenting "an appearance of dilapidated grandeur".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoxe1995b303-42)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** [Henry](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England")'s statue is generally considered to be of poor quality; [John Newman](/wiki/John_Newman_(architectural_historian) "John Newman (architectural historian)") thought it "incongruous",[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2000401-45) Jo Darke called it "decidedly-bad",[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDarke1991141-46) while the local historian [Keith Kissack](/wiki/Keith_Kissack "Keith Kissack") criticised it in two separate books, describing it as, "rather deplorable",[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKissack200333-47) and "pathetic...like a hypochondriac inspecting his thermometer".[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKissack197894-48)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-52)** The most famous of Henry V's Welsh supporters was [Dafydd Gam](/wiki/Dafydd_Gam "Dafydd Gam"). Shakespeare's character, [Fluellen](/wiki/Fluellen "Fluellen"), who appears in *[Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_(play) "Henry V (play)")* and has been suggested as being modelled on Gam, reminds the king; "If your Majesty is remembered of it, the Welshmen did good service in a garden where leeks did grow, wearing leeks in their [Monmouth caps](/wiki/Monmouth_cap "Monmouth cap"), which your Majesty knows, to this hour is an honourable badge of the service, and I do believe, your Majesty takes no scorn to wear the leek upon Saint Tavy's day".[[43]](#cite_note-51)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-55)** Coflein's entry for the battle site notes the traditional ascription to the hill but records that archaeological investigations have not uncovered evidence to support the claim.[[45]](#cite_note-auto4-54)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-98)** [Angus Buchanan (VC)](/wiki/Angus_Buchanan_(VC) "Angus Buchanan (VC)"), who was born and died at [Coleford, Gloucestershire](/wiki/Coleford,_Gloucestershire "Coleford, Gloucestershire"), served as head boy of [Monmouth School](/wiki/Monmouth_School_for_Boys "Monmouth School for Boys") and unveiled the school's war memorial in 1921. He is commemorated in the school's Buchanan House.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKissack1995106–108-95) [William Charles Williams](/wiki/William_Charles_Williams "William Charles Williams"), born out of the county but raised in Chepstow, was posthumously awarded a [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") for bravery in the [Gallipoli campaign](/wiki/Gallipoli_campaign "Gallipoli campaign") in World War I. He is commemorated at the [Chepstow War Memorial](/wiki/Chepstow_War_Memorial "Chepstow War Memorial").[[86]](#cite_note-96)[[87]](#cite_note-97)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-130)** The pollution of the [River Wye](/wiki/River_Wye "River Wye") is primarily attributed to the large-scale [battery farming](/wiki/Battery_cage "Battery cage") of poultry, with an estimated 23 million birds being bred in the river catchment area in 2023.[[117]](#cite_note-128)[[118]](#cite_note-129)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-133)** The use of the name "Monmouthshire" rather than "Monmouth" for the area aroused some controversy; it was supported by the member of parliament (MP) for [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Monmouth (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Roger Evans](/wiki/Roger_Kenneth_Evans "Roger Kenneth Evans"), but opposed by [Paul Murphy](/wiki/Paul_Murphy,_Baron_Murphy_of_Torfaen "Paul Murphy, Baron Murphy of Torfaen"), MP for [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Torfaen (UK Parliament constituency)") (inside the historic county of Monmouthshire but being reconstituted as a separate unitary authority).[[120]](#cite_note-132)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-153)** [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams") served as [Bishop of Monmouth](/wiki/Bishop_of_Monmouth "Bishop of Monmouth") from 1991-2002 before elevation to the [Archbishopric of Wales](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Wales "Archbishop of Wales") and then of [Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury").[[139]](#cite_note-auto-152)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-163)** The [Department for Transport](/wiki/Department_for_Transport "Department for Transport") notes that the decline in road traffic usage between 2016 and 2022 was almost entirely due to the dramatic fall in usage due to movement restrictions during the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom").[[148]](#cite_note-auto2-162)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-172)** The closest university to Monmouthshire is the [campus](/wiki/University_of_South_Wales#Newport "University of South Wales") of the [University of South Wales](/wiki/University_of_South_Wales "University of South Wales") at [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales").[[156]](#cite_note-171)
